does: This paper systematically catalogs jailbreak defenses across three intervention levels and proposes three novel defense strategies. The first employs prompt-level techniques including sanitization and paraphrasing to detect and neutralize adversarial inputs before they reach the model. The second uses inference-time logit steering to reinforce refusal behavior by manipulating activations in safety-sensitive model layers. The third leverages the MetaGPT framework to enforce structured role-based collaboration and domain constraints, effectively compartmentalizing task execution. Experiments on benchmark jailbreak datasets demonstrate substantial reductions in attack success rates, with the agent-based defense achieving complete mitigation, establishing empirical evidence for the effectiveness of these layered defense mechanisms.
connects: This work builds on growing research into adversarial robustness of language models and safety mechanisms designed to prevent misuse. It extends prior work on prompt injection attacks and adversarial prompting by systematizing defenses across multiple levels, responding to foundational work on model alignment and instruction-following safety. The logit steering approach connects to recent research on interpretability-based interventions and probing model internals to understand safety representations. The domain-specific agent defense draws on work applying structured frameworks like hierarchical task decomposition to control model behavior, while the taxonomy approach relates to earlier survey efforts that map the landscape of LLM vulnerabilities and defensive techniques across the field.
enables: This research opens several future directions for robust LLM deployment. The taxonomy provides a foundation for developing more sophisticated defenses that combine multiple intervention points, potentially inspiring hybrid approaches that leverage complementary strengths of prompt, model, and training-level strategies. The logit steering technique could be extended toward understanding which model components encode safety commitments, facilitating better interpretability research. The agent-based defense framework points toward using structured delegation and role-based reasoning as a general pattern for constraining model outputs in high-stakes applications. Additionally, the empirical trade-offs identified between defense effectiveness and utility suggest future work investigating defenses that preserve helpfulness while blocking adversarial exploitation, advancing the broader goal of building trustworthy AI systems deployable in real-world contexts.
